content.problemStatement:
Ram and Shyam are playing a game of Truth and Dare. In this game, Shyam will ask Ram to perform tasks of two types:


Truth task: Ram has to truthfully answer a question.
Dare task: Ram has to perform a given task.
Each task is described by an integer. (If a truth task and a dare task are described by the same integer, they are still different tasks.) You are given four lists of tasks:


Tr,1,Tr,2,…,Tr,trT_{r, 1}, T_{r, 2}, \dots, T_{r, t_r}Tr,1​,Tr,2​,…,Tr,tr​​: the truth tasks Ram can perform.
Dr,1,Dr,2,…,Dr,drD_{r, 1}, D_{r, 2}, \dots, D_{r, d_r}Dr,1​,Dr,2​,…,Dr,dr​​: the dare tasks Ram can perform.
Ts,1,Ts,2,…,Ts,tsT_{s, 1}, T_{s, 2}, \dots, T_{s, t_s}Ts,1​,Ts,2​,…,Ts,ts​​: the truth tasks Shyam can ask Ram to perform.
Ds,1,Ds,2,…,Ds,dsD_{s, 1}, D_{s, 2}, \dots, D_{s, d_s}Ds,1​,Ds,2​,…,Ds,ds​​: the dare tasks Shyam can ask Ram to perform.
Note that the elements of these lists are not necessarily distinct, each task may be repeated any number of times in each list.

Shyam wins the game if he can find a task Ram cannot perform. Ram wins if he performs all tasks Shyam asks him to. Find the winner of the game.

Let's take an example where Ram can perform truth tasks 333, 222 and 555 and dare tasks 222 and 100100100, and Shyam can give him truth tasks 222 and 333 and a dare task 100100100. We can see that whichever truth or dare tasks Shyam asks Ram to perform, Ram can easily perform them, so he wins. However, if Shyam can give him dare tasks 333 and 100100100, then Ram will not be able to perform dare task 333, so Shyam wins.
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. The description of T test cases follows. The first line of each test case contains a single integer tr denoting the number of truth tasks Ram can perform. The second line contains tr space-separated integers Tr,1, Tr,2, …, Tr,tr. The third line contains a single integer dr denoting the number of dare tasks Ram can perform. The fourth line contains dr space-separated integers Dr,1, Dr,2, …, Dr,dr. The fifth line contains a single integer ts denoting the number of truth tasks Shyam can ask Ram to perform. The sixth line contains ts space-separated integers Ts,1, Ts,2, …, Ts,ts. The seventh line contains a single integer ds denoting the number of dare tasks Shyam can ask Ram to perform. The eighth line contains ds space-separated integers Ds,1, Ds,2, …, Ds,ds.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing the string "yes" if Ram wins the game or "no" otherwise.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: Ram's truth tasks are [1,2] and his dare tasks are [1,3,2]. Shyam's truth tasks are [2] and his dare tasks are [3,2]. Ram can perform all tasks Shyam gives him.
Example case 2: Ram's truth tasks are [1,2] and his dare tasks are [1,3,2]. Shyam's truth tasks are [2] and his dare tasks are [3,2,4]. If Shyam asks Ram to perform dare task 4, Ram will not be able to do it.

solution.approach: To determine the winner of the game, we need to check if Ram can perform all tasks that Shyam can ask him to. We can do this by creating sets of Ram's truth and dare tasks and checking if all of Shyam's tasks are in these sets. If any of Shyam's tasks are not in Ram's sets, then Shyam wins. Otherwise, Ram wins.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * (tr + dr + ts + ds)) — we iterate over each test case and for each test case, we iterate over the tasks of Ram and Shyam.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(tr + dr + ts + ds) — we store the tasks of Ram and Shyam in sets.
